locked_facts: Energy oscillates between electric field in C and magnetic field in L
At t=0: all energy in capacitor (E_C = Q₀²/2C), zero in inductor
At t=T/4: all energy in inductor (E_L = LI_max²/2), zero in capacitor
Total energy E = Q₀²/2C = LI_max²/2 — conserved throughout
Current: i(t) = -Q₀ω₀ sin(ω₀t) = I_max sin(ω₀t) where I_max = Q₀ω₀
Analogy: mass-spring system with E_electric ↔ potential energy, E_magnetic ↔ kinetic energy
truth_statements: Total energy in ideal LC circuit is conserved — no dissipation
When capacitor is fully charged, inductor current is zero
When inductor current is maximum, capacitor charge is zero
LC oscillation frequency does not depend on initial charge or energy

aha_moment.what_to_show: Show E_C(t) and E_L(t) on the same graph. E_C = (Q₀²/2C)cos²(ω₀t) decreases from maximum. E_L = (LI²/2) increases from zero. Their sum is always constant = Q₀²/2C. The energy sloshes perfectly between them like a pendulum — except here it is between electric and magnetic fields.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: E_C in orange, E_L in blue, E_total in green (flat horizontal line). The total never changes while the individual energies oscillate. This is conservation of energy made visible.

simulation_states.label: Initial state — capacitor fully charged
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Capacitor charged to Q₀. No current. E_C = maximum shown on right panel. E_L = 0. Electric field lines shown between capacitor plates.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: All energy is stored as electric field energy in the capacitor. The inductor has no current, no magnetic field.

simulation_states.label: Capacitor discharging — current building
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Current begins to flow. Capacitor charge decreasing. Inductor has growing magnetic field (shown as field lines around inductor coil). E_C falling, E_L rising on graph.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: As capacitor discharges, the energy does not disappear — it transfers to the inductor's magnetic field. Energy is conserved.

simulation_states.label: Maximum current — the aha moment
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Capacitor fully discharged (Q = 0). Current at maximum I_max. E_C = 0, E_L = maximum. Energy is now entirely in the inductor's magnetic field.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: All electric energy has converted to magnetic energy. Now the inductor's field will drive current to recharge the capacitor in the opposite polarity — the oscillation continues.

simulation_states.label: Full cycle — analogy to pendulum
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Complete animation of one full oscillation. Alongside, a pendulum oscillating: KE at bottom (analogous to E_L at max current), PE at top (analogous to E_C at full charge).
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: LC oscillation IS the electrical pendulum. Same mathematics, same energy exchange, same frequency formula structure: ω = 1/√(LC) vs ω = √(g/L) for pendulum.

common_misconceptions.student_belief: LC oscillations will die out over time because current flowing through the inductor must lose energy
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: In an ideal LC circuit with zero resistance, oscillations continue indefinitely — no energy loss. Real circuits have small resistance and do damp over time, but ideal LC is lossless.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show the total energy E_total = E_C + E_L as a flat horizontal line even as individual energies oscillate. The flatness IS the conservation of energy.

trigger_keywords: charge current both zero same time
common_misconceptions.student_belief: Charge on capacitor and current in the circuit are in phase — both maximum at the same time
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: q = Q₀cos(ω₀t) and i = -Q₀ω₀sin(ω₀t). They are 90° out of phase. When charge is maximum (capacitor fully charged), current is zero. When current is maximum, charge is zero.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show q(t) and i(t) on the same graph. Their peaks are displaced by exactly T/4. Circle the moments where q = Q₀ (I = 0) and where I = I_max (q = 0) — make the 90° phase difference explicit.
